# Lesson Scene Prompt Guide

Use this with [design-guide.md](/Users/daquanfreeman/Documents/Code%20School/code-school/docs/product/design-guide.md), [design-system.md](/Users/daquanfreeman/Documents/Code%20School/code-school/docs/product/design-system.md), and [mascot-midjourney-prompts.md](/Users/daquanfreeman/Documents/Code%20School/code-school/docs/product/mascot-midjourney-prompts.md).

This guide separates each lesson into two production assets:

- `Background Prompt`: a Midjourney prompt for non-interactive habitat art only

- `Interactive Layer Build Prompt`: an implementation prompt for building the React/Next.js interactive lesson component, using Motion for UI animation and PixiJS only inside the lesson interaction area

## Shared Rules

- Background prompts are for art generation only.

- Interactive layer prompts are for engineering and implementation only. They are not image prompts.

- Interactive prompts should define mechanics, tappable objects, local state, feedback, completion conditions, and how the lesson maps to the concept.

- Keep the component layer visually aligned to the design system: rounded shapes, clear focus states, one primary interaction, child-readable feedback, and strong separation between decorative background and teachable foreground.

- Use React/Next.js for the lesson shell, Motion for general UI animation and state transitions, and PixiJS only inside the interactive lesson canvas or scene area.

## Recommended Component Patterns

- Arrays, shelves, baskets, stacks, queues, cards, and slot-based lessons:
  - Use normal React/Next.js DOM layout for the visible objects.
  - Use Motion for hover, reveal, swap, bounce, highlight, and state transitions.
  - Add PixiJS only for optional overlays like pointer trails, glow sweeps, connector lines, or celebratory particles.

- Linked lists and pointer-heavy structure lessons:
  - Use React/Next.js DOM nodes for the list items and labels.
  - Use Motion for focus, movement, and step transitions.
  - Use PixiJS only for connectors, arrows, pointer traces, loop highlights, and path emphasis.

- Grid lessons:
  - Start with a React/Next.js DOM grid for cells so the logic stays maintainable and accessible.
  - Use Motion for cell focus, state changes, and reveal timing.
  - Use PixiJS only when the lesson needs path drawing, area sweeps, island grouping, or richer cell-to-cell highlight effects.

- Tree and graph lessons:
  - Use React/Next.js for the page shell, support strip, controls, and mascot wrapper.
  - Use PixiJS as the core renderer inside the lesson scene area for nodes, branches, edges, routes, and traversal emphasis.
  - Use Motion around the shell and panel-level transitions, not as the primary graph or tree renderer.

- Generic concept lessons or intro scenes:
  - Stay DOM-first with React/Next.js and Motion.
  - Introduce PixiJS only if the concept truly benefits from richer spatial feedback, animated traces, or layered highlight behavior.

## Chapter Stack Recommendations

- Two Pointers:
  - React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for pointer trails, highlight sweeps, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
- Sliding Windows:
  - React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for moving window overlays, highlight bands, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
- Binary Search:
  - React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for range fades, midpoint sweeps, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
- Stacks:
  - React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for stack traces, glow pulses, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
- Sort and Search:
  - React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for swap traces, pointer lines, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
- Hash Maps And Sets:
  - React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for key-to-bucket traces, lookup glows, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
- Heaps:
  - React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for heap relationship lines, reorder traces, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
- Intervals:
  - React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for overlap bands, merge traces, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
- Prefix Sums:
  - React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for running-total trails, highlight sweeps, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
- Dynamic Programming:
  - React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for dependency traces, path highlights, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
- Greedy:
  - React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for decision traces, route emphasis, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
- Bit Manipulation:
  - React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for bit glows, switch pulses, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
- Linked Lists:
  - React/Next.js lesson module with DOM-based nodes, Motion for UI transitions, and PixiJS only for linked-node connectors, pointer traces, and loop emphasis.
- Fast and Slow Pointers:
  - React/Next.js lesson module with DOM-based nodes, Motion for UI transitions, and PixiJS only for linked-node connectors, pointer traces, and loop emphasis.
- Graphs:
  - React/Next.js lesson module with Motion for shell and UI transitions, plus a PixiJS scene core inside the lesson area for node-edge maps, traversal paths, and route animation.
- Trees:
  - React/Next.js lesson module with Motion for shell and UI transitions, plus a PixiJS scene core inside the lesson area for branches, nodes, depth traversal, and path emphasis.
- Tries:
  - React/Next.js lesson module with Motion for shell and UI transitions, plus a PixiJS scene core inside the lesson area for word branches, prefix paths, and node emphasis.
- Backtracking:
  - React/Next.js lesson module with Motion for shell and UI transitions, plus a PixiJS scene core inside the lesson area for branching paths, undo traces, and route emphasis.

## Backtracking

### Introduction to Backtracking

- Habitat: `Maze of Choices`

- Mascot: `Scout the Rabbit`

#### Background Prompt

```txt
Maze of Choices, non-interactive background plate for a children's coding lesson, for the lesson "Introduction to Backtracking", soft storybook vector habitat, flat illustration, rounded low-detail shapes, light neutral foundations, warm cream and pale sky base, restrained bright accents, three depth layers, far layer establishes the world softly with gentle hills, pale trees, and faint sky glow, middle layer uses quiet environmental motifs such as soft shrubs, a distant stream, and rounded bridge shapes with reduced contrast so the center never competes with the lesson, near-ground layer creates a calm open stage for a separate interactive layer with one clear foreground interaction corridor and a readable starting branch zone, winding path shapes may be suggested in the environment but should stay visually secondary, incomplete, and soft-edged so they do not feel like finished puzzle routes, leave clean mascot space near the bottom-right edge or lower corner without blocking the main branch area, background only, no mascot, no tappable objects, no arrows, no counters, no markers, no tokens, no targets, no active paths, no puzzle pieces, no text, no UI, no buttons, no watermark, no photorealism, no busy clutter, safe empty space for top header and bottom support strip, calm educational atmosphere, designed to support the lesson habitat without directly showing the puzzle logic, reduce visual pull in the central middle distance, push any large scenic props such as bridges, trees, or waterfalls farther back atmospherically or reduce their contrast, keep non-essential paths low contrast, and preserve a foreground zone where the interactive maze layer can read clearly, follow the Code School design system with soft neutral backgrounds, rounded storybook forms, and low-detail scenery that stays visually secondary --v 7 --style raw --ar 16:9 --stylize 80
```

#### Interactive Layer Build Prompt

```txt
Build the interactive lesson layer for "Introduction to Backtracking" in the `Maze of Choices` habitat.
Technology target: React/Next.js lesson module with Motion for shell and UI transitions, plus a PixiJS scene core inside the lesson area for branching paths, undo traces, and route emphasis.
This is an implementation prompt for React/Next.js + Motion + PixiJS, not an image-generation prompt.

Goals
- Teach the concept: backtracking means choose, explore, undo, and try again when there are many possible paths.
- Use `Scout the Rabbit` as the guide character in the foreground teaching layer
- Keep the component visually separate from the pre-rendered background plate
- Make the cause-and-effect of the algorithm visible through interaction

Foreground Objects To Build
- A branching maze with several decision points
- A current path ribbon showing the choices made so far
- An undo arrow returning to the last branch point

Primary Learner Actions
- Choose a branch
- Explore the path
- Step back with undo and try again

Support Language
- "Choose one path."
- "If it does not work, undo and try another."

Required Mechanics
- Each tappable object must expose hover, focus, pressed, correct, and retry states where relevant
- Only one primary interaction target should be visually emphasized at a time
- Correct actions should visibly advance the lesson model
- Incorrect actions should pause, highlight the relevant object, and show one calm hint instead of a harsh error state
- The mascot should respond at decision moments, not continuously animate over the lesson
- Use Motion for shell transitions, prompt reveals, feedback states, and CTA motion
- Use PixiJS only for the lesson-area rendering where it adds value, such as paths, highlights, particles, connectors, or draggable / tappable scene objects
- Keep the interaction logic and lesson state in React, not inside PixiJS rendering code

State Model To Implement
- Track the active step or scene state for the current lesson interaction
- Track which foreground objects are selectable, selected, disabled, completed, or incorrect
- Track hint / replay / retry state separately from lesson completion state
- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

Interaction Flow To Implement
- Scout introduces the maze and the first choice point.
- The learner chooses one branch and sees the current path ribbon grow.
- If the path fails or finishes, the undo arrow returns to the last decision point.
- A different branch is then explored.
- The support strip names this cycle as choose, explore, undo.

Visual / Motion Rules
- The maze should make decision points feel inviting, not stressful. The current path ribbon needs to show the exact choices already made. The undo arrow should feel calm and helpful because backtracking is not failure, it is part of the plan.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions
- Use PixiJS for lesson-area visuals like path traces, connectors, glow pulses, highlight sweeps, or lightweight particles only when they clarify the interaction
- Keep decorative depth secondary to the tappable teaching objects
- Do not bake text into PixiJS sprites or art; text should remain in React UI overlays where possible

Component Boundaries
- Scene Card
- Current-path ribbon
- Undo arrow
- Decision chips
- Start-lesson CTA

Completion Criteria
- The learner can complete the lesson using the intended interaction path without extra explanation
- The active object, next action, and result of the action are always visually obvious
- The component teaches the rule through interaction, not just through static text
- Keyboard and pointer interactions are both supported for all tappable objects

Implementation Notes
- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A branching maze with several decision points, A current path ribbon showing the choices made so far, and An undo arrow returning to the last branch point. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "Choose one path." and "If it does not work, undo and try another.". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Choose a branch, Explore the path, and Step back with undo and try again, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story. The objects in the world stand in for the data structure, the movement in the world stands in for the algorithm step, and the learner choice stands in for the reasoning the algorithm is using. When those three things stay aligned, the child can understand the concept through action instead of only through explanation.
- Navigation affordances expected around this layer: Replay, Hint, and Start lesson 1
- The final build should feel like a lesson mechanic, not a static illustration
```

### Lesson 1: Generate All Subsets

- Habitat: `Choice Basket Grove`

- Mascot: `Scout the Rabbit`

#### Background Prompt

```txt
Choice Basket Grove, non-interactive background plate for a children's coding lesson, for the lesson "Lesson 1: Generate All Subsets", soft storybook vector habitat, flat illustration, rounded low-detail shapes, light neutral foundations, warm cream and pale sky base, restrained bright accents, three depth layers, far layer establishes the world softly, middle layer uses quiet environmental motifs such as rolling grass shapes, small flowers or shrubs, and soft tree or hedge silhouettes, near-ground layer creates a calm open stage for a separate interactive layer with one clear foreground interaction corridor, preserve a clean readable center or lower foreground where the interactive objects can sit without competing with the environment, leave optional mascot space near one lower side or corner so a guide character can appear without blocking the lesson, background only, no mascot, no tappable objects, no arrows, no counters, no markers, no tokens, no targets, no active paths, no puzzle pieces, no text, no UI, no buttons, no watermark, no photorealism, no busy clutter, safe empty space for top header and bottom support strip, calm educational atmosphere, designed to support the lesson habitat without directly showing the puzzle logic, keep middle-distance details soft and lower contrast than the teaching layer, push any large scenic props such as bridges, trees, gates, arches, waterfalls, towers, or big habitat landmarks farther back atmospherically or reduce their contrast so they never become the focal point, and ensure any environment shapes that resemble routes, nodes, grids, shelves, branches, puzzle boards, or play surfaces remain clearly decorative rather than active, follow the Code School design system with soft neutral backgrounds, rounded storybook forms, and low-detail scenery that stays visually secondary --v 7 --style raw --ar 16:9 --stylize 80
```

#### Interactive Layer Build Prompt

```txt
Build the interactive lesson layer for "Lesson 1: Generate All Subsets" in the `Choice Basket Grove` habitat.
Technology target: React/Next.js lesson module with Motion for shell and UI transitions, plus a PixiJS scene core inside the lesson area for branching paths, undo traces, and route emphasis.
This is an implementation prompt for React/Next.js + Motion + PixiJS, not an image-generation prompt.

Goals
- Teach the concept: each number gives two choices: take it or skip it.
- Use `Scout the Rabbit` as the guide character in the foreground teaching layer
- Keep the component visually separate from the pre-rendered background plate
- Make the cause-and-effect of the algorithm visible through interaction

Foreground Objects To Build
- A row of number baskets
- A branching decision tree with take and skip paths
- A result shelf collecting finished subsets

Primary Learner Actions
- Take the current number
- Skip the current number
- Save the finished subset when choices run out

Support Language
- "For each number, choose take or skip."
- "Every full path becomes one subset."

Required Mechanics
- Each tappable object must expose hover, focus, pressed, correct, and retry states where relevant
- Only one primary interaction target should be visually emphasized at a time
- Correct actions should visibly advance the lesson model
- Incorrect actions should pause, highlight the relevant object, and show one calm hint instead of a harsh error state
- The mascot should respond at decision moments, not continuously animate over the lesson
- Use Motion for shell transitions, prompt reveals, feedback states, and CTA motion
- Use PixiJS only for the lesson-area rendering where it adds value, such as paths, highlights, particles, connectors, or draggable / tappable scene objects
- Keep the interaction logic and lesson state in React, not inside PixiJS rendering code

State Model To Implement
- Track the active step or scene state for the current lesson interaction
- Track which foreground objects are selectable, selected, disabled, completed, or incorrect
- Track hint / replay / retry state separately from lesson completion state
- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

Interaction Flow To Implement
- Scout stands at the first number basket.
- The learner chooses take or skip.
- The current subset changes depending on that choice.
- When all numbers have been decided, the subset moves to the result shelf.
- The learner backtracks to try the other branch.

Visual / Motion Rules
- The take and skip branches should be visually balanced so both feel like real choices. The current subset should stay visible as a growing basket list. The result shelf should fill with finished subsets in a friendly, orderly way.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions
- Use PixiJS for lesson-area visuals like path traces, connectors, glow pulses, highlight sweeps, or lightweight particles only when they clarify the interaction
- Keep decorative depth secondary to the tappable teaching objects
- Do not bake text into PixiJS sprites or art; text should remain in React UI overlays where possible

Component Boundaries
- Scene Card
- Take / skip branch cards
- Current-subset tray
- Result shelf
- Hint card

Completion Criteria
- The learner can complete the lesson using the intended interaction path without extra explanation
- The active object, next action, and result of the action are always visually obvious
- The component teaches the rule through interaction, not just through static text
- Keyboard and pointer interactions are both supported for all tappable objects

Implementation Notes
- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A row of number baskets, A branching decision tree with take and skip paths, and A result shelf collecting finished subsets. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "For each number, choose take or skip." and "Every full path becomes one subset.". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Take the current number, Skip the current number, and Save the finished subset when choices run out, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story. The objects in the world stand in for the data structure, the movement in the world stands in for the algorithm step, and the learner choice stands in for the reasoning the algorithm is using. When those three things stay aligned, the child can understand the concept through action instead of only through explanation.
- Navigation affordances expected around this layer: Replay, Hint, and Check answer
- The final build should feel like a lesson mechanic, not a static illustration
```

### Lesson 2: Generate All Permutations

- Habitat: `Parade Line Plaza`

- Mascot: `Scout the Rabbit`

#### Background Prompt

```txt
Parade Line Plaza, non-interactive background plate for a children's coding lesson, for the lesson "Lesson 2: Generate All Permutations", soft storybook vector habitat, flat illustration, rounded low-detail shapes, light neutral foundations, warm cream and pale sky base, restrained bright accents, three depth layers, far layer establishes the world softly, middle layer uses quiet environmental motifs such as open gathering space, banner or tent silhouettes, and clear central stage area, near-ground layer creates a calm open stage for a separate interactive layer with one clear foreground interaction corridor, preserve a clean readable center or lower foreground where the interactive objects can sit without competing with the environment, leave optional mascot space near one lower side or corner so a guide character can appear without blocking the lesson, background only, no mascot, no tappable objects, no arrows, no counters, no markers, no tokens, no targets, no active paths, no puzzle pieces, no text, no UI, no buttons, no watermark, no photorealism, no busy clutter, safe empty space for top header and bottom support strip, calm educational atmosphere, designed to support the lesson habitat without directly showing the puzzle logic, keep middle-distance details soft and lower contrast than the teaching layer, push any large scenic props such as bridges, trees, gates, arches, waterfalls, towers, or big habitat landmarks farther back atmospherically or reduce their contrast so they never become the focal point, and ensure any environment shapes that resemble routes, nodes, grids, shelves, branches, puzzle boards, or play surfaces remain clearly decorative rather than active, follow the Code School design system with soft neutral backgrounds, rounded storybook forms, and low-detail scenery that stays visually secondary --v 7 --style raw --ar 16:9 --stylize 80
```

#### Interactive Layer Build Prompt

```txt
Build the interactive lesson layer for "Lesson 2: Generate All Permutations" in the `Parade Line Plaza` habitat.
Technology target: React/Next.js lesson module with Motion for shell and UI transitions, plus a PixiJS scene core inside the lesson area for branching paths, undo traces, and route emphasis.
This is an implementation prompt for React/Next.js + Motion + PixiJS, not an image-generation prompt.

Goals
- Teach the concept: permutations are built by choosing which unused number goes in the next spot.
- Use `Scout the Rabbit` as the guide character in the foreground teaching layer
- Keep the component visually separate from the pre-rendered background plate
- Make the cause-and-effect of the algorithm visible through interaction

Foreground Objects To Build
- A row of empty parade spots
- A set of unused number cards
- A used-card tray and a finished-parade shelf

Primary Learner Actions
- Choose an unused number
- Place it in the next parade spot
- Undo and try a different card

Support Language
- "Pick one unused card for the next spot."
- "Undo the pick so other orders can be tried."

Required Mechanics
- Each tappable object must expose hover, focus, pressed, correct, and retry states where relevant
- Only one primary interaction target should be visually emphasized at a time
- Correct actions should visibly advance the lesson model
- Incorrect actions should pause, highlight the relevant object, and show one calm hint instead of a harsh error state
- The mascot should respond at decision moments, not continuously animate over the lesson
- Use Motion for shell transitions, prompt reveals, feedback states, and CTA motion
- Use PixiJS only for the lesson-area rendering where it adds value, such as paths, highlights, particles, connectors, or draggable / tappable scene objects
- Keep the interaction logic and lesson state in React, not inside PixiJS rendering code

State Model To Implement
- Track the active step or scene state for the current lesson interaction
- Track which foreground objects are selectable, selected, disabled, completed, or incorrect
- Track hint / replay / retry state separately from lesson completion state
- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

Interaction Flow To Implement
- Scout starts with all cards unused.
- The learner chooses one card for the next open spot.
- The card moves into the parade line and becomes used.
- When the line is full, the permutation moves to the finished shelf.
- The learner undoes the last choice and tries another unused card.

Visual / Motion Rules
- The empty parade spots should make order matter visually. Used cards should clearly move out of the unused pile and into the current arrangement. Finished permutations should line up neatly on the shelf.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions
- Use PixiJS for lesson-area visuals like path traces, connectors, glow pulses, highlight sweeps, or lightweight particles only when they clarify the interaction
- Keep decorative depth secondary to the tappable teaching objects
- Do not bake text into PixiJS sprites or art; text should remain in React UI overlays where possible

Component Boundaries
- Scene Card
- Unused-card tray
- Parade spot row
- Finished-permutation shelf
- Hint card

Completion Criteria
- The learner can complete the lesson using the intended interaction path without extra explanation
- The active object, next action, and result of the action are always visually obvious
- The component teaches the rule through interaction, not just through static text
- Keyboard and pointer interactions are both supported for all tappable objects

Implementation Notes
- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A row of empty parade spots, A set of unused number cards, and A used-card tray and a finished-parade shelf. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "Pick one unused card for the next spot." and "Undo the pick so other orders can be tried.". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Choose an unused number, Place it in the next parade spot, and Undo and try a different card, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story. The objects in the world stand in for the data structure, the movement in the world stands in for the algorithm step, and the learner choice stands in for the reasoning the algorithm is using. When those three things stay aligned, the child can understand the concept through action instead of only through explanation.
- Navigation affordances expected around this layer: Replay, Hint, and Check answer
- The final build should feel like a lesson mechanic, not a static illustration
```

### Lesson 3: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number

- Habitat: `Phone Garden Gate`

- Mascot: `Scout the Rabbit`

#### Background Prompt

```txt
Phone Garden Gate, non-interactive background plate for a children's coding lesson, for the lesson "Lesson 3: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number", soft storybook vector habitat, flat illustration, rounded low-detail shapes, light neutral foundations, warm cream and pale sky base, restrained bright accents, three depth layers, far layer establishes the world softly, middle layer uses quiet environmental motifs such as rolling grass shapes, small flowers or shrubs, and soft tree or hedge silhouettes, near-ground layer creates a calm open stage for a separate interactive layer with one clear foreground interaction corridor, preserve a clean readable center or lower foreground where the interactive objects can sit without competing with the environment, leave optional mascot space near one lower side or corner so a guide character can appear without blocking the lesson, background only, no mascot, no tappable objects, no arrows, no counters, no markers, no tokens, no targets, no active paths, no puzzle pieces, no text, no UI, no buttons, no watermark, no photorealism, no busy clutter, safe empty space for top header and bottom support strip, calm educational atmosphere, designed to support the lesson habitat without directly showing the puzzle logic, keep middle-distance details soft and lower contrast than the teaching layer, push any large scenic props such as bridges, trees, gates, arches, waterfalls, towers, or big habitat landmarks farther back atmospherically or reduce their contrast so they never become the focal point, and ensure any environment shapes that resemble routes, nodes, grids, shelves, branches, puzzle boards, or play surfaces remain clearly decorative rather than active, follow the Code School design system with soft neutral backgrounds, rounded storybook forms, and low-detail scenery that stays visually secondary --v 7 --style raw --ar 16:9 --stylize 80
```

#### Interactive Layer Build Prompt

```txt
Build the interactive lesson layer for "Lesson 3: Letter Combinations of a Phone Number" in the `Phone Garden Gate` habitat.
Technology target: React/Next.js lesson module with Motion for shell and UI transitions, plus a PixiJS scene core inside the lesson area for branching paths, undo traces, and route emphasis.
This is an implementation prompt for React/Next.js + Motion + PixiJS, not an image-generation prompt.

Goals
- Teach the concept: each phone digit gives a small set of letters, and backtracking tries every letter choice step by step.
- Use `Scout the Rabbit` as the guide character in the foreground teaching layer
- Keep the component visually separate from the pre-rendered background plate
- Make the cause-and-effect of the algorithm visible through interaction

Foreground Objects To Build
- A row of phone digits with their letter petals beneath them
- A growing word vine showing the current combination
- A result board collecting completed words

Primary Learner Actions
- Choose a letter from the current digit
- Grow the word vine
- Save a completed combination

Support Language
- "Each digit opens a small letter set."
- "Pick one letter, then move to the next digit."

Required Mechanics
- Each tappable object must expose hover, focus, pressed, correct, and retry states where relevant
- Only one primary interaction target should be visually emphasized at a time
- Correct actions should visibly advance the lesson model
- Incorrect actions should pause, highlight the relevant object, and show one calm hint instead of a harsh error state
- The mascot should respond at decision moments, not continuously animate over the lesson
- Use Motion for shell transitions, prompt reveals, feedback states, and CTA motion
- Use PixiJS only for the lesson-area rendering where it adds value, such as paths, highlights, particles, connectors, or draggable / tappable scene objects
- Keep the interaction logic and lesson state in React, not inside PixiJS rendering code

State Model To Implement
- Track the active step or scene state for the current lesson interaction
- Track which foreground objects are selectable, selected, disabled, completed, or incorrect
- Track hint / replay / retry state separately from lesson completion state
- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

Interaction Flow To Implement
- Scout reveals the first digit and its letter petals.
- The learner chooses one letter and adds it to the word vine.
- The process repeats for the next digit.
- When every digit has been used, the full combination goes to the result board.
- The learner backtracks to try a different letter choice.

Visual / Motion Rules
- The letter petals should make the digit-to-letter mapping feel playful and easy to scan. The word vine should grow one letter at a time so the combination feels built, not just listed. Keep the result board visible but secondary.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions
- Use PixiJS for lesson-area visuals like path traces, connectors, glow pulses, highlight sweeps, or lightweight particles only when they clarify the interaction
- Keep decorative depth secondary to the tappable teaching objects
- Do not bake text into PixiJS sprites or art; text should remain in React UI overlays where possible

Component Boundaries
- Scene Card
- Digit petals
- Word vine
- Result board
- Hint card

Completion Criteria
- The learner can complete the lesson using the intended interaction path without extra explanation
- The active object, next action, and result of the action are always visually obvious
- The component teaches the rule through interaction, not just through static text
- Keyboard and pointer interactions are both supported for all tappable objects

Implementation Notes
- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A row of phone digits with their letter petals beneath them, A growing word vine showing the current combination, and A result board collecting completed words. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "Each digit opens a small letter set." and "Pick one letter, then move to the next digit.". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Choose a letter from the current digit, Grow the word vine, and Save a completed combination, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story. The objects in the world stand in for the data structure, the movement in the world stands in for the algorithm step, and the learner choice stands in for the reasoning the algorithm is using. When those three things stay aligned, the child can understand the concept through action instead of only through explanation.
- Navigation affordances expected around this layer: Replay, Hint, and Check answer
- The final build should feel like a lesson mechanic, not a static illustration
```

```

#### Interactive Layer Build Prompt

```txt
Build the interactive lesson layer for "Lesson 4: Word Search" in the `Letter Trail Field` habitat.
Technology target: React/Next.js lesson module with Motion for shell and UI transitions, plus a PixiJS scene core inside the lesson area for branching paths, undo traces, and route emphasis.
This is an implementation prompt for React/Next.js + Motion + PixiJS, not an image-generation prompt.

Goals
- Teach the concept: we can search a word in a grid by trying paths and undoing when a path stops working.
- Use `Scout the Rabbit` as the guide character in the foreground teaching layer
- Keep the component visually separate from the pre-rendered background plate
- Make the cause-and-effect of the algorithm visible through interaction

Foreground Objects To Build
- A letter grid with one active path highlighted
- A target word badge above the grid
- A visited-cell trail showing which squares are in the current path

Primary Learner Actions
- Start from a possible first letter
- Explore neighboring cells
- Undo visited cells when the path fails

Support Language
- "Move to neighboring letters only."
- "If the path breaks, step back and try another direction."

Required Mechanics
- Each tappable object must expose hover, focus, pressed, correct, and retry states where relevant
- Only one primary interaction target should be visually emphasized at a time
- Correct actions should visibly advance the lesson model
- Incorrect actions should pause, highlight the relevant object, and show one calm hint instead of a harsh error state
- The mascot should respond at decision moments, not continuously animate over the lesson
- Use Motion for shell transitions, prompt reveals, feedback states, and CTA motion
- Use PixiJS only for the lesson-area rendering where it adds value, such as paths, highlights, particles, connectors, or draggable / tappable scene objects
- Keep the interaction logic and lesson state in React, not inside PixiJS rendering code

State Model To Implement
- Track the active step or scene state for the current lesson interaction
- Track which foreground objects are selectable, selected, disabled, completed, or incorrect
- Track hint / replay / retry state separately from lesson completion state
- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

Interaction Flow To Implement
- Scout finds a possible starting letter in the grid.
- The learner follows one neighboring path trying to match the target word.
- The visited-cell trail grows as letters match.
- If the path fails, the trail rolls back one step at a time.
- The learner tries a new direction or a new start cell.

Visual / Motion Rules
- The grid needs to be large and readable, with the current path easy to trace. Visited cells should be visibly marked so the learner knows they cannot be reused in the same path. Failed-path steps should fade gently when undone.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions
- Use PixiJS for lesson-area visuals like path traces, connectors, glow pulses, highlight sweeps, or lightweight particles only when they clarify the interaction
- Keep decorative depth secondary to the tappable teaching objects
- Do not bake text into PixiJS sprites or art; text should remain in React UI overlays where possible

Component Boundaries
- Scene Card
- Target word badge
- Visited-cell trail
- Grid highlight
- Hint card

Completion Criteria
- The learner can complete the lesson using the intended interaction path without extra explanation
- The active object, next action, and result of the action are always visually obvious
- The component teaches the rule through interaction, not just through static text
- Keyboard and pointer interactions are both supported for all tappable objects

Implementation Notes
- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A letter grid with one active path highlighted, A target word badge above the grid, and A visited-cell trail showing which squares are in the current path. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "Move to neighboring letters only." and "If the path breaks, step back and try another direction.". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Start from a possible first letter, Explore neighboring cells, and Undo visited cells when the path fails, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story. The objects in the world stand in for the data structure, the movement in the world stands in for the algorithm step, and the learner choice stands in for the reasoning the algorithm is using. When those three things stay aligned, the child can understand the concept through action instead of only through explanation.
- Navigation affordances expected around this layer: Replay, Hint, and Check answer
- The final build should feel like a lesson mechanic, not a static illustration
```

- Make the cause-and-effect of the algorithm visible through interaction

- Open-count and close-count meters
- A validity shield that dims when a move would break the rules

Primary Learner Actions
- Add an opening parenthesis
- Add a closing parenthesis when allowed
- Save a finished valid string

Support Language
- "You can only close if there is something open to match."
- "Build only valid strings as you go."

Required Mechanics
- Each tappable object must expose hover, focus, pressed, correct, and retry states where relevant
- Only one primary interaction target should be visually emphasized at a time
- Correct actions should visibly advance the lesson model
- Incorrect actions should pause, highlight the relevant object, and show one calm hint instead of a harsh error state

- Track hint / replay / retry state separately from lesson completion state
- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

Interaction Flow To Implement
- Scout begins with an empty string.
- The learner adds opening parentheses while there are still openings left.
- Closing parentheses become available only when the string stays valid.
- A finished valid string is saved when both counts reach the goal.
- The learner backtracks to try a different valid path.

Visual / Motion Rules
- The open-count and close-count meters should make the rule visible rather than hidden in logic. The validity shield should gently block invalid moves instead of letting the learner wander into broken strings. Finished strings should appear on a neat result banner.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions
- Use PixiJS for lesson-area visuals like path traces, connectors, glow pulses, highlight sweeps, or lightweight particles only when they clarify the interaction
- Keep decorative depth secondary to the tappable teaching objects

- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A stage showing a growing parentheses string, Open-count and close-count meters, and A validity shield that dims when a move would break the rules. - Update the current sum
- Save or undo based on whether the sum hits, misses, or passes the target

- "Keep adding choices toward the goal."
- "If the sum gets too big, undo and try another path."

- If the sum goes too high, the last choice is undone.
- The process continues until all useful paths are explored.

Visual / Motion Rules

- The active object, next action, and result of the action are always visually obvious
- The component teaches the rule through interaction, not just through static text
- Keyboard and pointer interactions are both supported for all tappable objects

Implementation Notes
- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through One focused backtracking challenge, A visible current path and next choice point, and A result badge area above the scene. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "What should you try next?" and "If it cannot work, where should you undo?". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Predict the next choice, Solve one short backtracking challenge, and Explain why the path stayed valid or had to be undone, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story. The objects in the world stand in for the data structure, the movement in the world stands in for the algorithm step, and the learner choice stands in for the reasoning the algorithm is using. When those three things stay aligned, the child can understand the concept through action instead of only through explanation.
- Navigation affordances expected around this layer: Replay, Hint, and Finish challenge
- The final build should feel like a lesson mechanic, not a static illustration
```

#### Interactive Layer Build Prompt

```txt
Build the interactive lesson layer for "Introduction to Binary Search" in the `Maple Library` habitat.
Technology target: React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for range fades, midpoint sweeps, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
This is an implementation prompt for React/Next.js + Motion + PixiJS, not an image-generation prompt.

Goals
- Teach the concept: sorted order lets us check the middle and throw away half of the remaining choices.
- Use `Milo the Owl` as the guide character in the foreground teaching layer
- Keep the component visually separate from the pre-rendered background plate
- Make the cause-and-effect of the algorithm visible through interaction

Foreground Objects To Build
- A long sorted bookshelf of numbered books
- Left, right, and middle bookmarks
- A faded overlay on the half we can throw away

Primary Learner Actions
- Jump to the middle book
- Compare it to the target
- Fade out the half that cannot contain the answer

Support Language
- "Because it is sorted, the middle gives us a clue."
- "After one check, we can ignore half."

Required Mechanics
- Each tappable object must expose hover, focus, pressed, correct, and retry states where relevant
- Only one primary interaction target should be visually emphasized at a time
- Correct actions should visibly advance the lesson model
- Incorrect actions should pause, highlight the relevant object, and show one calm hint instead of a harsh error state
- The mascot should respond at decision moments, not continuously animate over the lesson
- Use Motion for shell transitions, prompt reveals, feedback states, and CTA motion
- Use PixiJS only for the lesson-area rendering where it adds value, such as paths, highlights, particles, connectors, or draggable / tappable scene objects
- Keep the interaction logic and lesson state in React, not inside PixiJS rendering code

- Track which foreground objects are selectable, selected, disabled, completed, or incorrect
- Track hint / replay / retry state separately from lesson completion state
- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

Interaction Flow To Implement
- Milo introduces a sorted shelf and a target book.
- The learner jumps to the middle bookmark.
- A compare bubble explains whether the middle is too small, too big, or correct.
- The impossible half fades away.
- The scene repeats once more so the learner feels the shrinking search area.

Visual / Motion Rules
- The library metaphor should make ordered searching feel calm and smart. Left, right, and middle bookmarks must stay visible the whole time. When a half gets removed, fade it softly rather than making it disappear abruptly so children can still understand what was discarded.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions
- Use PixiJS for lesson-area visuals like path traces, connectors, glow pulses, highlight sweeps, or lightweight particles only when they clarify the interaction
- Keep decorative depth secondary to the tappable teaching objects
- Do not bake text into PixiJS sprites or art; text should remain in React UI overlays where possible

Component Boundaries

- Left / right / middle bookmarks
- Compare bubble
- Discarded-half overlay
- Start-lesson CTA

Completion Criteria
- The learner can complete the lesson using the intended interaction path without extra explanation
- The active object, next action, and result of the action are always visually obvious
- The component teaches the rule through interaction, not just through static text
- Keyboard and pointer interactions are both supported for all tappable objects

- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A long sorted bookshelf of numbered books, Left, right, and middle bookmarks, and A faded overlay on the half we can throw away. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "Because it is sorted, the middle gives us a clue." and "After one check, we can ignore half.". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Jump to the middle book, Compare it to the target, and Fade out the half that cannot contain the answer, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story. The objects in the world stand in for the data structure, the movement in the world stands in for the algorithm step, and the learner choice stands in for the reasoning the algorithm is using. - This screen should mirror the previous lesson closely so the difference between first and last is easy to compare. The right-search arrow needs to be the main change. Repeated copies should stay clearly visible.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions
- Use PixiJS for lesson-area visuals like path traces, connectors, glow pulses, highlight sweeps, or lightweight particles only when they clarify the interaction
- Keep decorative depth secondary to the tappable teaching objects
- Do not bake text into PixiJS sprites or art; text should remain in React UI overlays where possible

Component Boundaries
- Scene Card
- Candidate badge
- Right-search arrow
- Left / right / middle markers
- Hint card

Completion Criteria
- The learner can complete the lesson using the intended interaction path without extra explanation
- The active object, next action, and result of the action are always visually obvious
- The component teaches the rule through interaction, not just through static text
- Keyboard and pointer interactions are both supported for all tappable objects

Implementation Notes
- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A sorted shelf with repeated matching books, A candidate badge on one found match, and A right-search arrow showing we should keep checking later copies. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "A match is only the start." and "For the last copy, keep searching right.". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Find a match, Save it as a candidate, and Move left inward to the right half, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story. The objects in the world stand in for the data structure, the movement in the world stands in for the algorithm step, and the learner choice stands in for the reasoning the algorithm is using. When those three things stay aligned, the child can understand the concept through action instead of only through explanation.
- Navigation affordances expected around this layer: Replay, Hint, and Check answer
- The final build should feel like a lesson mechanic, not a static illustration
```

#### Interactive Layer Build Prompt

```txt
Build the interactive lesson layer for "Lesson 5: Integer Square Root" in the `Square Garden` habitat.
Technology target: React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for range fades, midpoint sweeps, and lightweight lesson-area effects.
This is an implementation prompt for React/Next.js + Motion + PixiJS, not an image-generation prompt.

Goals
- Teach the concept: binary search can work on a number range, not just on an array of values.
- Use `Milo the Owl` as the guide character in the foreground teaching layer
- Keep the component visually separate from the pre-rendered background plate
- Make the cause-and-effect of the algorithm visible through interaction

Foreground Objects To Build
- A number path from 1 up to the target range
- A middle guess marker
- A square tile board that shows mid × mid

Primary Learner Actions
- Pick the middle guess
- Compute mid times mid
- Move the range left or right

Support Language
- "Guess the middle number."
- "Check whether its square is too small or too large."

Required Mechanics
- Each tappable object must expose hover, focus, pressed, correct, and retry states where relevant
- Only one primary interaction target should be visually emphasized at a time
- Correct actions should visibly advance the lesson model
- Incorrect actions should pause, highlight the relevant object, and show one calm hint instead of a harsh error state
- The mascot should respond at decision moments, not continuously animate over the lesson
- Use Motion for shell transitions, prompt reveals, feedback states, and CTA motion
- Use PixiJS only for the lesson-area rendering where it adds value, such as paths, highlights, particles, connectors, or draggable / tappable scene objects
- Keep the interaction logic and lesson state in React, not inside PixiJS rendering code

- Track the active step or scene state for the current lesson interaction
- Track which foreground objects are selectable, selected, disabled, completed, or incorrect
- Track hint / replay / retry state separately from lesson completion state
- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

Interaction Flow To Implement
- Milo marks the current number range.
- The learner chooses the middle guess.
- A square tile board builds `mid × mid`.
- The range shrinks depending on whether the square is too small or too large.
- The final answer is the largest guess whose square does not go over.

Visual / Motion Rules
- The square tile board should make `mid × mid` visual, not just numeric. This helps children understand why the comparison matters. The range path should be simple and evenly spaced so number binary search feels familiar.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions
- Use PixiJS for lesson-area visuals like path traces, connectors, glow pulses, highlight sweeps, or lightweight particles only when they clarify the interaction
- Keep decorative depth secondary to the tappable teaching objects
- Do not bake text into PixiJS sprites or art; text should remain in React UI overlays where possible

Component Boundaries

- Guess marker
- Square tile board
- Left / right / middle range markers
- Hint card

Completion Criteria

- The active object, next action, and result of the action are always visually obvious
- The component teaches the rule through interaction, not just through static text
- Keyboard and pointer interactions are both supported for all tappable objects

Implementation Notes
- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A number path from 1 up to the target range, A middle guess marker, and A square tile board that shows mid × mid. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "Guess the middle number." and "Check whether its square is too small or too large.". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Pick the middle guess, Compute mid times mid, and Move the range left or right, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story. The objects in the world stand in for the data structure, the movement in the world stands in for the algorithm step, and the learner choice stands in for the reasoning the algorithm is using. - Track hint / replay / retry state separately from lesson completion state
- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

Interaction Flow To Implement
- Blinky introduces the bit switches as tiny on/off lights.
- The learner toggles switches and watches the number value change.
- The place-value ladder explains how those bits add up.
- One operator card demonstrates a simple bit rule, such as XOR canceling.
- The support strip explains that bit manipulation is about working with these tiny switches directly.

Visual / Motion Rules
- The switchboard needs to feel playful and readable, with each bit clearly labeled as `0` or `1`. The place-value ladder should make binary value feel concrete. Operator cards should show simple before-and-after state changes rather than dense truth tables.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions
- Use PixiJS for lesson-area visuals like path traces, connectors, glow pulses, highlight sweeps, or lightweight particles only when they clarify the interaction
- Keep decorative depth secondary to the tappable teaching objects
- Do not bake text into PixiJS sprites or art; text should remain in React UI overlays where possible

Component Boundaries
- Scene Card
- Bit-switch row
- Place-value ladder
- Operator demo cards
- Start-lesson CTA

Completion Criteria
- The learner can complete the lesson using the intended interaction path without extra explanation
- The active object, next action, and result of the action are always visually obvious
- The component teaches the rule through interaction, not just through static text
- Keyboard and pointer interactions are both supported for all tappable objects

Implementation Notes
- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A row of glowing on/off bit switches, A place-value ladder labeled 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, and Operator cards for AND, OR, XOR, and shifts. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "A bit is a tiny off/on switch." and "Many switches together can build a number.". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Toggle bit switches on and off, Watch the number value update, and Test one operator card on two bit rows, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story. The objects in the world stand in for the data structure, the movement in the world stands in for the algorithm step, and the learner choice stands in for the reasoning the algorithm is using. When those three things stay aligned, the child can understand the concept through action instead of only through explanation.
- Navigation affordances expected around this layer: Replay, Hint, and Start lesson 1
- The final build should feel like a lesson mechanic, not a static illustration
```

- Each tappable object must expose hover, focus, pressed, correct, and retry states where relevant
- Only one primary interaction target should be visually emphasized at a time
- Correct actions should visibly advance the lesson model
- Incorrect actions should pause, highlight the relevant object, and show one calm hint instead of a harsh error state
- The mascot should respond at decision moments, not continuously animate over the lesson
- Use Motion for shell transitions, prompt reveals, feedback states, and CTA motion
- Use PixiJS only for the lesson-area rendering where it adds value, such as paths, highlights, particles, connectors, or draggable / tappable scene objects
- Keep the interaction logic and lesson state in React, not inside PixiJS rendering code

State Model To Implement
- Track the active step or scene state for the current lesson interaction
- Track which foreground objects are selectable, selected, disabled, completed, or incorrect
- Track hint / replay / retry state separately from lesson completion state
- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

Interaction Flow To Implement
- Blinky starts at zero with count zero.
- The learner moves upward through the number ladder.
- A helper arrow points from a smaller known answer.
- The next count badge is filled using that smaller result.
- The support strip explains that counting bits can build step by step.

Visual / Motion Rules
- The number ladder should feel incremental and easy to follow. Count badges should be simple and consistent. The helper arrow should reinforce that this lesson uses saved smaller answers, not full recounting every time.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions
- Use PixiJS for lesson-area visuals like path traces, connectors, glow pulses, highlight sweeps, or lightweight particles only when they clarify the interaction
- Keep decorative depth secondary to the tappable teaching objects
- Do not bake text into PixiJS sprites or art; text should remain in React UI overlays where possible

Component Boundaries
- Scene Card
- Number ladder
- Count badges
- Helper arrows
- Hint card

Completion Criteria
- The learner can complete the lesson using the intended interaction path without extra explanation
- The active object, next action, and result of the action are always visually obvious
- The component teaches the rule through interaction, not just through static text
- Keyboard and pointer interactions are both supported for all tappable objects

Implementation Notes
- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A ladder of numbers from 0 up to n, A count badge under each number, and A helper arrow showing how one smaller answer helps the next. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "Smaller bit answers can help build bigger ones." and "Reuse what you already counted.". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Walk up the number ladder, Fill the count badge for each number, and Use the helper arrow from a related smaller number, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story. The objects in the world stand in for the data structure, the movement in the world stands in for the algorithm step, and the learner choice stands in for the reasoning the algorithm is using. When those three things stay aligned, the child can understand the concept through action instead of only through explanation.
- Navigation affordances expected around this layer: Replay, Hint, and Check answer
- The final build should feel like a lesson mechanic, not a static illustration
```

- Use `Dot the Beaver` as the guide character in the foreground teaching layer
- Keep the component visually separate from the pre-rendered background plate
- Make the cause-and-effect of the algorithm visible through interaction

Foreground Objects To Build
- A number path with DP stones from 0 up to n
- Base-case stones for 0 and 1
- Two helper arrows feeding into the next Fibonacci stone

Primary Learner Actions
- Read the two previous values
- Add them to fill the next slot
- Continue until n is reached

Support Language
- "Each new number uses the two before it."
- "Save the old answers so you can build the next one."

Required Mechanics
- Each tappable object must expose hover, focus, pressed, correct, and retry states where relevant
- Only one primary interaction target should be visually emphasized at a time
- Correct actions should visibly advance the lesson model
- Incorrect actions should pause, highlight the relevant object, and show one calm hint instead of a harsh error state
- The mascot should respond at decision moments, not continuously animate over the lesson
- Use Motion for shell transitions, prompt reveals, feedback states, and CTA motion
- Use PixiJS only for the lesson-area rendering where it adds value, such as paths, highlights, particles, connectors, or draggable / tappable scene objects
- Keep the interaction logic and lesson state in React, not inside PixiJS rendering code

State Model To Implement
- Track the active step or scene state for the current lesson interaction
- Track which foreground objects are selectable, selected, disabled, completed, or incorrect
- Track hint / replay / retry state separately from lesson completion state
- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

Interaction Flow To Implement
- Dot starts with `F(0)` and `F(1)` on the base shelf.
- The learner adds the two previous values to create the next one.
- The next DP stone fills in.
- The process repeats until the target `n` is reached.
- The support strip names the recurrence in simple language.

Visual / Motion Rules
- Keep the path clean and repetitive so the pattern is obvious. The two helper arrows should always point from `i - 1` and `i - 2` into the next DP stone. Base cases should stay highlighted the whole time.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions
- Use PixiJS for lesson-area visuals like path traces, connectors, glow pulses, highlight sweeps, or lightweight particles only when they clarify the interaction
- Keep decorative depth secondary to the tappable teaching objects
- Do not bake text into PixiJS sprites or art; text should remain in React UI overlays where possible

Component Boundaries
- Scene Card
- DP stone row
- Helper arrows
- Base-case shelf
- Hint card

Completion Criteria
- The learner can complete the lesson using the intended interaction path without extra explanation
- The active object, next action, and result of the action are always visually obvious
- The component teaches the rule through interaction, not just through static text
- Keyboard and pointer interactions are both supported for all tappable objects

Implementation Notes
- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A number path with DP stones from 0 up to n, Base-case stones for 0 and 1, and Two helper arrows feeding into the next Fibonacci stone. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "Each new number uses the two before it." and "Save the old answers so you can build the next one.". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Read the two previous values, Add them to fill the next slot, and Continue until n is reached, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story. The objects in the world stand in for the data structure, the movement in the world stands in for the algorithm step, and the learner choice stands in for the reasoning the algorithm is using. When those three things stay aligned, the child can understand the concept through action instead of only through explanation.
- Navigation affordances expected around this layer: Replay, Hint, and Check answer
- The final build should feel like a lesson mechanic, not a static illustration
```

- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

- Those counts combine to fill the next stair badge.
- The process repeats upward.
- The top stair shows the total number of ways.

Visual / Motion Rules
- The staircase should make the one-step and two-step moves obvious. Each stair badge should display the number of ways to get there. Keep the motion playful so the counting feels like a path-building activity.
- Use Motion for UI-level movement like fades, scale, bounce, and panel transitions

- The interaction should follow a consistent learning loop: look at the scene, identify the active object, make one small move, then watch the scene explain why that move mattered. This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A staircase with one- and two-step hop arrows, A DP badge on each stair showing ways to reach it, and Base-case signs at the first steps. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "To reach this stair, you came from one step below or two steps below." and "Add those saved counts together.". - "Save the smaller total cost."

- Each tappable object must expose hover, focus, pressed, correct, and retry states where relevant

- The DP badge for that stair is updated.
- The lesson ends with the minimum total cost to reach the top.

Visual / Motion Rules

Technology target: React/Next.js DOM-first lesson module with Motion for UI transitions, using PixiJS only for dependency traces, path highlights, and lightweight lesson-area effects.

- Teach the concept: at each house we choose between taking this house or skipping it and keeping the better saved total.

- Keep the component visually separate from the pre-rendered background plate
- Make the cause-and-effect of the algorithm visible through interaction

- A take-or-skip decision card at each house
- A best-total badge showing the safest highest amount so far

- Compare take vs skip totals
- Choose the larger saved answer
- Move to the next house

Support Language
- "If you take this house, you must skip the neighbor."
- "Save the better of take or skip."

- Each tappable object must expose hover, focus, pressed, correct, and retry states where relevant

- Track progress toward the lesson-specific completion condition
- Track temporary feedback state for success, retry, and guided correction

- Dot stands at the current house and shows its money bag.
- The learner compares the take option to the skip option.
- The best-total badge saves the larger answer.
- The process continues house by house.
- The support strip explains that DP stores the best answer up to each house.

Visual / Motion Rules
- The houses should clearly show their money values, and neighboring houses should feel linked so the no-adjacent rule is visible. The take-or-skip card must make both options readable. Avoid crime-heavy visuals; keep it abstract and child-safe.

This keeps the world and the concept aligned, because the learner is not only reading about the rule but also seeing it happen through A line of houses with money bags, A take-or-skip decision card at each house, and A best-total badge showing the safest highest amount so far. The support strip should act like a translator between the visual world and the lesson vocabulary. It should stay short, concrete, and synchronized with the scene state, especially when it reinforces ideas such as "If you take this house, you must skip the neighbor." and "Save the better of take or skip.". The action zone should stay tightly focused on Compare take vs skip totals, Choose the larger saved answer, and Move to the next house, so the child always knows what to do next and never has to hunt across the screen for the primary interaction. Most importantly, the scene should coincide with the lesson by making the algorithm feel like a visible cause-and-effect story.
